title: scenario_dre_callout
description: A frontline officer arrests a driver for drug-impaired operation but is not DRE-certified. The officer must coordinate a Drug Recognition Expert callout, maintain Charter protections during the evaluation, and facilitate a blood demand under Criminal Code s.320.28.

timeline.title: Arrest Made
timeline.description: Driver arrested for drug-impaired operation. Signs of drug impairment present but officer is not certified as a Drug Recognition Expert.

timeline.title: Transport to Detachment
timeline.description: Driver transported to detachment. Charter rights read. Driver provided opportunity to contact counsel.

timeline.title: DRE Requested
timeline.description: Officer requests a Drug Recognition Expert attend the detachment. DRE estimated arrival: 40 minutes.

timeline.title: DRE Evaluation Begins
timeline.description: DRE officer begins the 12-step evaluation. Driver is cooperative. Vitals, pupil size, and muscle tone are recorded.

timeline.title: DRE Opinion Formed
timeline.description: DRE concludes the driver is impaired by a CNS depressant. DRE makes demand for a blood sample under Criminal Code s.320.28 to confirm drug presence.

timeline.title: Blood Sample Taken
timeline.description: Blood drawn by qualified medical professional. Sample sent to Health Canada approved lab for toxicological analysis.

node-1.title: Drug Impairment Arrest — DRE Needed
node-1.situation: You have arrested a driver for drug-impaired operation under Criminal Code s.320.14(1)(a) following a traffic stop. The driver has pinpoint pupils, muscle rigidity, slowed speech, and a slow pulse — indicators consistent with opioid impairment. You have administered the SFST and the driver failed. You are not a Drug Recognition Expert. The driver is at the detachment, having spoken with duty counsel. You must ensure the investigation proceeds lawfully and completely.
node-1.question: What is your correct course of action?

options.label: A
options.text: Contact the on-call Drug Recognition Expert (DRE) and request their attendance at the detachment to conduct the 12-step DRE evaluation. While waiting, document all your observations in detail: pupil size in different lighting, vital signs you are able to note, muscle tone, speech, and behaviour. Ensure the driver remains at the detachment lawfully under the arrest authority.

options.label: B
options.text: Conduct the 12-step DRE evaluation yourself using the standardised form, since you have seen it done before and the driver is cooperative.

options.label: C
options.text: Release the driver pending further investigation since you cannot complete the DRE evaluation yourself and no DRE is immediately available.

node-2.title: DRE Evaluation in Progress
node-2.situation: The DRE officer has arrived and begins the 12-step evaluation. At step 6, the DRE requests that the driver perform the divided attention tests. The driver, now aware of the process, states: 'I want to speak to my lawyer again before I do any more tests.' The driver has already had a meaningful opportunity to speak with duty counsel prior to the DRE evaluation. The DRE evaluation is part of the lawful arrest process under the Criminal Code.
node-2.question: How do you and the DRE handle this request?

options.label: A
options.text: Advise the driver that their right to counsel has already been satisfied. The DRE evaluation is not a separate event triggering a new right to counsel — it is part of the ongoing investigation following a lawful arrest. Document the driver's request and the response. The DRE continues with the evaluation. If the driver refuses to participate, document refusal for each step.

options.label: B
options.text: Halt the DRE evaluation entirely and allow the driver to make a second call to a lawyer, potentially allowing significant time to pass and affecting the blood toxicology results.

options.label: C
options.text: Tell the driver that complying with the DRE evaluation is optional and they are free to refuse without consequence.

node-3.title: Blood Demand Under s.320.28
node-3.situation: The DRE completes all 12 steps of the evaluation and forms the opinion that the driver is impaired by a CNS depressant, consistent with opioid use. Based on this opinion, the DRE makes a demand for a blood sample under Criminal Code s.320.28, which allows a qualified DRE to demand blood when they have reasonable grounds to believe a drug has impaired the driver. The demand must be carried out by a qualified person — a nurse or physician must draw the blood.
node-3.question: How is the blood demand correctly executed?

options.label: A
options.text: The DRE formally reads the blood demand to the driver under Criminal Code s.320.28, advising that refusal is a criminal offence under s.320.15. Arrange for a registered nurse or physician to attend the detachment or transport the driver to a medical facility to draw two blood samples. Maintain chain of custody: samples are sealed, labelled with the officer's badge number, and couriered to a Health Canada approved toxicology laboratory.

options.label: B
options.text: Draw the blood sample yourself using a first aid kit to avoid delays, since time is critical to preserving evidence.

options.label: C
options.text: Skip the blood demand since the DRE opinion alone is sufficient evidence and a blood test is only optional.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: Blood samples were drawn by a registered nurse and submitted to the Health Canada approved laboratory. Toxicological analysis confirmed morphine at a concentration consistent with impairment. The driver was charged under Criminal Code s.320.14(1)(a) (impaired operation — drug). The DRE officer testified as an expert witness. Your thorough documentation of arrest grounds, Charter compliance, and coordination of the DRE evaluation and blood demand provided the foundation for a successful prosecution. The 12-step DRE evaluation and confirmatory blood evidence together constitute a robust evidentiary package under Canadian drug-impaired driving law.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
